text: Five trocars, including four robotic trocars, are inserted into the abdominal cavity. The surgeon performs an episcopic evaluation, noting significant visceral obesity and a narrow pelvis. The transdental approach is initiated for the TME, starting at the dentate line with a Lone Star retractor. The internal anal sphincter is resected for approximately two centimeters. The TEO endoscopic platform is inserted, and the rectum is insufflated with CO₂. Additional resection is performed endoscopically using a bipolar device.

The surgeon performs a third viperspring dissection, revealing the anatomical plane between the rectum and prostate anteriorly, and reaching the presacral space posteriorly. The rectum is then occluded endoscopically using a viperspring suture with the endostitch device.

The surgeon completes the circumferential mobilization of the rectum and mesorectum, sharply dissecting anteriorly between the anterior rectal wall and posterior prostate using cautery and a bipolar device. The dissection proceeds laterally, preserving the mesorectum and lateral pelvic-side wall nerves, and heads cephaloid towards the peritoneal cavity. Anteriorly, the prostate and seminal vesicles are preserved, while posteriorly, dissection follows the presacral plane.

The surgeon reaches and divides the peritoneal reflection, confirming entry into the peritoneal cavity from below. Mesorectal and rectal dissection are completed circumferentially. The robotic arms are docked, and the abdominal portion of the total mesorectal excision (TME) is performed robotically. The sigmoid colon is elevated to place the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) under tension, which is scored with cautery and dissected using the robotic arms.

The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer to divide the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and dissect the sigmoid mesentery. Medial to lateral mobilization is completed, followed by pelvic dissection to free residual rectal and mesorectal attachments using a transdental endoscopic approach. The previous dissection plane is reached, and the rectum is completely freed using a robotic abdominal approach.

The surgeon removes the robotic arms and performs splenic flexure mobilization using a laparoscopic approach. The inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is dissected, and the lateral attachments of the proximal colon are divided. After completing the laparoscopic plantar fascia takedown, the specimen is transiently exteriorized with laparoscopic assistance, ensuring no twists in the colon or mesentery. The specimen is transected transanally, followed by a hand-sewn coloanal anastomosis. A laparoscopic loop ileostomy is created, and a pelvic drain is placed.
